coredata.dc:title: Rapid microwave assisted synthesis of N-doped CQDs for highly selective ‘turn-off’ sensing of Bismuth(III) ions in wastewater
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Anoushka,
Rani, Manviri
Shanker, Uma
coredata.dc:description: The growing utilization of bismuth across diverse industries and pharmaceuticals has raised concerns about its environmental accumulation and the potential for neurotoxic and nephrotoxic effects in humans. Currently available techniques for its detection are often complex and costly, making CQDs an appealing alternative due to their low toxicity, cost-effectiveness, and ease of synthesis. Herein, a novel, environmentally sustainable one-pot microwave-assisted method for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) has been reported for the selective and sensitive detection of bismuth ions (Bi3+). The synthesized N-CQDs, with an impressive quantum yield of 47.5 %, exhibited remarkable stability and were applied as fluorescent sensors for detecting Bi3+ ions, achieving highly selective detection through fluorescence quenching. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.365 μM within a linear concentration range of 0.95–61.5 μM, with the quenching mechanism identified as dynamic quenching via a photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process. The practical applicability of this sensing platform was demonstrated through the analysis of various real-world samples, including tap water, industrial wastewater and agricultural runoff, with recovery rates ranging from 98.7 % to 101.6 %. The applications of these N-CQDs as fluorescent ink and in anti-counterfeiting were demonstrated. Further, the N-CQDs were combined with an RGB analysis tool to detect Bi3+. This method-notable for its simplicity, cost-efficiency, and scalability-offers a sustainable and effective approach for detecting Bi3+ ions in various environmental contexts, presenting a significant advancement in the field of metal ion sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-CQDs
Metal ion sensing
Microwave synthesis
Fluorescence quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
Detection of Bi3+ ions in real samples
Application as fluorescent ink and in anti-counterfeiting

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Dr Uma Shanker and Dr Manviri Rani are grateful to DST-SERB, New Delhi (Sanction order no. EEQ/2023/000196 & CRG/2023/002740) for funding this project. Ms Anoushka thanks the Ministry of Education (MoE) in New Delhi for a research fellowship. Characterization support from Material Research Centre MNIT Jaipur, IIC, NIT Jalandhar India, and TRFSM facility, IIT Bombay is greatly acknowledged.

Dr US and Dr MR are grateful to DST-SERB, New Delhi (Sanction order no. EEQ/2023/000196 & CRG/2023/002740) for funding this project. Ms A thanks the Ministry of Education (MoE) in New Delhi for a research fellowship. Characterization support from Material Research Centre MNIT Jaipur, IIC, NIT Jalandhar India, and TRFSM facility, IIT Bombay is greatly acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication of N-CQDs from citric acid and ethylenediamine via microwave method.
(a) HR-TEM image (Inset shows the SAED pattern) (b) size distribution histogram (c) XRD image (d) FTIR spectrum of N-CQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectra of N-CQDs (b)–(d) deconvoluted spectra of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s of N-CQDs.
(a) UV- absorption spectrum (b) FL emission spectrum of N-CQDs at excitation wavelengths ranging from 310 to 390 nm.
N-CQDs stability studies: effect of (a) pH, (b) storage time, (c) ionic strength of NaCl, and (d) temperature on their FL intensity.

(a) Change in fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs with the change in concentrations of Bi3+ (b) Stern−Volmer plot of N-CQDs (c) FL intensity ratio of the N-CQDs solutions with different metal ions (d) Interference study of N-CQDs solution containing Bi3+ ions and other metal ions (e) N-CQDs containing different analytes under UV-light irradiation.

(a) Fluorescence lifetime studies of N-CQDs before and after adding Bi3+ (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Bi3+ and excitation spectrum of N-CQDs. (c) Effect of temperature on quenching constant (d) FTIR spectra of N-CQDs before and after adding Bi3+ ions e) Schematic representation of Bi3+ ions causing fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs via electron transfer.
Photograph of words written using N-CQDs as fluorescent ink under a) natural light and b) UV radiation of 365 nm. c) Plot of G values v/s concentration of Bi3+(μM).
Some sensing applications of N-CQDs fabricated from different precursors.

row.entry: Methyl Glycine Diacetic acid Trisodium Salt (MGDA) and m-Phenylenediamine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Bi3+ in real samples using N-CQDs.
Comparison of the concentration of Bi3+ determined in real samples measured using FL spectroscopy and UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy.

head.ce:title: Rapid microwave assisted synthesis of N-doped CQDs for highly selective ‘turn-off’ sensing of Bismuth(III) ions in wastewater

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Nanomaterials green synthesis laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Dr B R Ambedkar National Institute of Technology Jalandhar, Punjab, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr B R Ambedkar National Institute of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The growing utilization of bismuth across diverse industries and pharmaceuticals has raised concerns about its environmental accumulation and the potential for neurotoxic and nephrotoxic effects in humans. Currently available techniques for its detection are often complex and costly, making CQDs an appealing alternative due to their low toxicity, cost-effectiveness, and ease of synthesis. Herein, a novel, environmentally sustainable one-pot microwave-assisted method for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) has been reported for the selective and sensitive detection of bismuth ions (Bi3+). The synthesized N-CQDs, with an impressive quantum yield of 47.5 %, exhibited remarkable stability and were applied as fluorescent sensors for detecting Bi3+ ions, achieving highly selective detection through fluorescence quenching. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.365 μM within a linear concentration range of 0.95–61.5 μM, with the quenching mechanism identified as dynamic quenching via a photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process. The practical applicability of this sensing platform was demonstrated through the analysis of various real-world samples, including tap water, industrial wastewater and agricultural runoff, with recovery rates ranging from 98.7 % to 101.6 %. The applications of these N-CQDs as fluorescent ink and in anti-counterfeiting were demonstrated. Further, the N-CQDs were combined with an RGB analysis tool to detect Bi3+. This method-notable for its simplicity, cost-efficiency, and scalability-offers a sustainable and effective approach for detecting Bi3+ ions in various environmental contexts, presenting a significant advancement in the field of metal ion sensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bismuth compounds boast a wide range of applications in various industries, including semiconductors, cosmetics, metallurgy, pigments, and pharmaceuticals. They are used in iron castings, electronics, lubricating oils and greases, and even as a reagent for sugar purification [1–4]. Bi has been reported for its antibacterial properties when incorporated into PEO (plasma electrolytic oxidation) coatings on valve metals [5]. Bismuth's presence in medicine has significantly increased, with applications in antibacterial treatments, anti-gastritis medications, anti-HIV agents, and radiotherapeutics [6–8]. Bismuth is also commonly used in cosmetic formulations, such as foundations and powders, to impart a radiant finish and long-lasting wear. One widely used form, bismuth oxychloride, is known to potentially irritate the skin and worsen conditions like acne [9]. This growing use has led to a rise in environmental bismuth levels, potentially increasing human and organism exposure. While generally considered safe for human health, the accumulation of bismuth in the brain and kidneys has been reported to trigger bismuth-induced neurotoxic side effects. Several documented cases have reported nephrotoxic and neurotoxic damage linked to the increased use of Bi3+-containing pharmaceutical formulations [1,3,8,10]. This growing concern has spurred the development of various methods for accurately determining Bi3+ levels in the environment and biological systems. Numerous techniques have been developed to detect trace levels of these ions. These include inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES), atomic fluorescence spectroscopy (AFS), electrochemical methods, hydride generation inductively-coupled plasma spectrometry, resonance light scattering, atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), and various stripping voltammetry methods [7–14]. While these methods offer high sensitivity and selectivity, they are often hindered by significant drawbacks, including high operational costs, the necessity of sophisticated instrumentation, lengthy pre-treatment steps, and the requirement for skilled operators [6,15,16].

Recently, fluorescence-based techniques have revolutionized metal ion detection by offering a user-friendly and cost-effective approach. Unlike traditional methods, fluorescence-based methods provide real-time results with high sensitivity and selectivity. This translates to faster analysis times and the ability to detect trace levels of metal ions with minimal interference [17–19]. Within the vast array of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) hold immense promise for heavy metal detection. These remarkable nanostructures offer several advantages over conventional semiconductor quantum dots. They boast superior properties like tuneable light emission, enhanced stability due to their smaller size, photo-bleaching resistance, and improved biocompatibility with lower toxicity [20–23]. This unique combination makes them ideal for developing field-useable sensors that are both sensitive and practical. CQDs can be synthesized using various techniques, such as laser ablation, subcritical or supercritical water method [22], arc discharge, electrooxidation, chemical oxidation, microwave-assisted pyrolysis, hydrothermal method, and ultrasonic synthesis [16,21,23] These techniques offer several benefits over other bottom-up methods. However, our work takes advantage of the remarkable efficiency of microwave-assisted synthesis. This technique outshines traditional approaches, like the hydrothermal method, by offering significantly faster reaction times. Microwave irradiation delivers superior heating efficiency, minimizing unwanted side reactions and byproducts. In contrast to conventional methods that often suffer from uneven heat distribution, microwaves provide rapid and uniform heating throughout the reaction mixture, ensuring a cleaner and more consistent final product [24–26]. A significant challenge with CQD research is boosting their photoluminescence (PL) intensity. Doping of CQDs with heteroatoms like S, N, B, and P or even metals like Ag and Au introduces defects and binding sites, ultimately leading to improved PL [27,28]. [29]. This doping approach also alters the electrical structure of CQDs, affecting charge carrier dynamics and increasing quantum efficiency [22,26]. Among these dopants, nitrogen-doping is favoured owing to its ease of integration with carbon atoms. Nitrogen's size (comparable to carbon) and electron configuration enable strong bonding. At the same time, its n-type donor properties may help prevent PL quenching to some extent, thus enhancing their stability [30,31]. Table 1 summarizes some of the diverse sensing applications of N-doped CQDs synthesized from various precursors, showcasing their versatility in detecting different analytes.

While N-CQDs boast a proven track record in analyte detection, a gap exists in their application for bismuth ion identification. Prior work that has been done does not specifically involve using N-CQDs for Bi3+ ion sensing (Table S1). For instance, Ramaswamy et al. [1] developed a dual-emission N-hydroxy 1,8-naphthalimide chemosensor, which showed a red shift in absorption and fluorescence enhancement for highly selective Bi3+ ions in neutral aqueous solutions, with a LOD of 0.58 μg/ml. Guo et al. [2] employed a method using the adsorption of bismuth-bromopyrogallol red at a carbon paste electrode to detect trace bismuth. Kumar et al. [3] designed a chemosensor, 8-methoxy-2-(thiophen-2-yl) quinolone (L), for Bi3+ detection, which showed colorimetric changes and fluorescence quenching in CH3CN: H2O (85:15, v/v). This research represents a groundbreaking approach, marking the first application of N-CQDs synthesized via microwave-assisted methods for the highly selective and sensitive detection of Bi3+ ions. Herein, we propose a simple and rapid one-pot microwave method to create stable N-CQDs utilizing citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors. This method stands out for its simplicity, cost-efficiency, and scalability, paving the way for large-scale production of N-CQDs. This one-pot approach eliminates hazardous chemicals and laborious procedures and yields water-soluble, highly stable N-CQDs with bright blue emission and an impressive quantum yield of 47.5 %.

Citric acid monohydrate, ethylenediamine, quinine sulphate, disodium EDTA and all metal salts (Cobalt chloride, Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate, Cupric nitrate trihydrate, Nickel chloride, Lead nitrate, Ferric chloride, Zinc chloride, Sodium arsenate, Manganese chloride tetrahydrate, Aluminium chloride, Cadmium chloride, Chromium nitrate nonahydrate) were acquired from Loba Chemie, India. Deionized (DI) water was employed in the experiment. All the reagents were of AR grade and used without additional purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-CQDs were synthesized using citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (En) as precursors. Fig. 1 illustrates the scheme for the synthesis of N-CQDs from these precursors using microwave method. Initially, 4 mmol of CA were dissolved in 10 mL of DI water. To introduce nitrogen doping, 3 mmol of En, dissolved in 10 mL of DI water, were slowly added to the CA solution. The mixture was then sonicated for 30 min to ensure thorough mixing and subsequently exposed to microwave radiation at 1200 W for 20 min. Post-irradiation, the solution was allowed to cool to room temperature and then diluted with 20 mL of DI water. The mixture was then filtered to remove any solid particles. The resulting dark brown solution was subjected to purification using a 1000 Da dialysis bag over a period of 48 h. The purified N-CQD solution was then stored at 4 °C, ensuring its stability for further use.

To evaluate the fluorescence characteristics of the N-CQDs, their quantum yield (QY) was determined using quinine sulphate (QS) as a reference (QY = 54 %). 0.1 M of QS was prepared using 0.01 M H2SO4 as the solvent and then used as a reference (QY = 54 %). The QY of N-CQDs was calculated using the following formula:
(1)
φ C
=
φ R
(
A c
A R
)
(
A b s
R
A b s
C
)
(
η R
η C
)
2
where “φ,” “A,” “Abs,” and “η” represent the QY, integral area of PL emission, absorbance, and refractive index, respectively. The subscripts “r” and “C” represent reference and N-CQDs, respectively (Fig. S1) [26].

To evaluate the selectivity and sensitivity of N-CQDs for Bi3+ ions, 500 μM solutions of various metal cations (Ni2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, As5+ and Pb2+) were prepared in DI water. Subsequently, each metal ion solution was introduced to the N-CQD working solution (15 μL of N-CQDs in 10 mL of DI water). The sensitivity was studied using a stock solution of 100 μΜ of Bi3+. The mixtures were allowed to equilibrate for 10 min to ensure stability. Fluorescence intensity measurements were then recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. All experimental procedures were conducted at room temperature to maintain consistency.

To assess the CQD sensor's practical use, we tested it with various real-world samples. Tap water and agricultural runoff from a local field were taken for analysis. The samples were first filtered to remove large particles and were diluted 10 folds to minimize particles that could interfere with the measurements. Due to the absence of Bi3+ ions in these samples, a recovery experiment was carried out by adding known amounts of Bi3+ (5–15 μM). Repeating the experiment three times, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated. Most studies have reported metal ion determination in spiked real samples; however, we have also analysed bismuth-containing samples in this work. To investigate Bi concentrations in industrial environments, we collected wastewater sample from a local valve manufacturing factory. To minimize matrix interference, the wastewater was filtered and subjected to a five-fold dilution before analysis. To assess the presence of Bi in consumer cosmetics, we analysed 4 mg of blush from a popular brand by dispersing it in water and then filtering it. All the measurements were carried out at neutral pH to align it with the optimal fluorescence response range of our N-CQDs. The bismuth concentrations in both the industrial wastewater and cosmetic samples were initially determined via FL spectroscopy using the as synthesized N-CQDs. These results were subsequently cross-validated using UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy.

HR-TEM determined the internal morphology and particle size of the N-CQDs. The HR-TEM images revealed that the N-CQDs were nearly spherical, with a narrow particle size distribution ranging from 3.7 to 8.4 nm and an average diameter of 5.64 ± 1.23 nm (Fig. 2 a and b). This data confirmed their monodisperse nature. Furthermore, the SAED pattern indicates a semi-amorphous structure of the N-CQDs (Fig. 2a(inset)). The amorphous form of the synthesized N-CQDs was further confirmed by XRD analysis (Fig. 2c). The XRD pattern showed a broad peak around 24°, indicating diffraction of the (002) crystal plane. This broad peak demonstrates the N-CQDs' amorphous form. The diffraction investigation indicated an interlayer spacing of 3.75 Å, higher than the average 3.4 Å found in bulk graphite, indicating the amorphous nature of the carbon dots. The tightly packed carbon atoms and the abundance of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the N-CQDs' surface are most likely the causes of this amorphous shape [26,41]. Since the XRD pattern only displayed the characteristic peaks of carbon dots, these results validate the structural integrity and purity of the N-CQDs.

The FT-IR analysis of N-CQDs reveals the presence of various functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, amide, alkyl, and aryl groups (Fig. 2d). A broad peak at 3200-3400 cm−1 indicates O–H/N–H stretching vibrations. A minor peak at 2936 cm−1 signifies C–H stretching vibrations associated with sp3 hybridized carbon atoms [30,42,43]. The amide I band at 1650 cm−1 is weaker than the amide II band (1540 cm−1), which suggests the presence of a pyridine-like structure within the carbon core of the N-CQDs, as the peak at 1540 cm−1 is a combination of CN stretching and amide II vibrations. The peak at 1360 cm−1 is attributed to C–N stretching, N–H bending, or CC stretching vibrations, and the existence of the C–O–C group is suggested by the peak at 1170 cm−1. These oxygenated and nitrogenous functional groups contribute to the hydrophilicity of the as-synthesized NCQDs [28,43–46]. Upon introducing Bi3+ ions into the N-CQD solution, a noticeable change appears in the IR spectrum (Fig. 6d). The decrease in the intensity of the O–H/N–H band implies that these groups might be involved in hydrogen bonding or electrostatic interaction with the Bi3+ ions. Additionally, the peaks in the amide regions decrease considerably, indicating a possible coordination interaction between the amide groups and the Bi3+ ions. The C–O stretching band (1170 cm−1) also shows a change in intensity after adding Bi3+, suggesting that oxygen-containing groups (like ethers) may bond or interact with the Bi3+ ions. Overall, the spectral analysis suggests that Bi3+ ions primarily interact with O–H/N–H, CO/CC, and amide functional groups on the surface of the NCQDs. These interactions likely involve hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, or coordination bonding between the lone pairs of oxygen/nitrogen and the positively charged Bi3+ ions.

The elemental composition and chemical state of various elements on N-CQDs' surface were ascertained using XPS. In the overall survey scanning spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. 3 a), the three main peaks at 283.95 eV, 401.02 eV, and 530.84 eV represent C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s respectively [31]. Three peaks at 284.6 eV, 286.12 eV, and 288.4 eV have been recognized in the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 3b) as belonging to the C–C/CC, C–O/C–N, and CO/CN groups, respectively [47]. The N 1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 3c) shows 3 peaks revealing the presence of N–H (399.2 eV), NC (400.4 eV), and N–C (401.5 eV) bonds [31,48]. In the spectrum for O 1s (Fig. 3d), the presence of C–OH/C–O–C groups is confirmed by the peak at ∼531.9 eV [21]. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis was also conducted to determine the sample's elemental composition (Fig. S2). The results revealed the presence of C, N, and O with respective weight percentages of 60.23 %, 15.15 %, and 24.62 %. Based on these findings, it can be concluded that nitrogen was successfully doped into CQDs to produce N-CQDs.

Both UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra were employed to explore the optical properties of N-CQDs. The UV–vis absorption spectrum is presented in Fig. 4 a, where a distinct absorption peak was observed at 352 nm. This peak is associated with the n to π electronic transition of CN/CO bonds, a characteristic feature of N-CQDs [48,49]. Such transitions play a pivotal role in the photophysical behaviour of N-CQDs, contributing to their absorption and emission capabilities. The fluorescence emission spectrum of N-CQDs was investigated at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 310 to 390 nm (Fig. 4b), revealing their tuneable fluorescence properties. Maximum emission was recorded at 460 nm when excited at 360 nm, a wavelength that produced the most intense fluorescence response. These findings illustrate the versatile optical behaviour of N-CQDs, which is influenced by the excitation source. Furthermore, the physical appearance of the CQDs was notable, displaying a brown colour under daylight while emitting a bright blue luminescence under UV light radiation at 365 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 1. The observed fluorescence emission is believed to result from photoinduced charge separation followed by surface site trapping, which promotes radiative recombination between electrons and holes, thereby emitting light [21,41]. This mechanism of charge separation and trapping is fundamental to understanding the photoluminescence of CQDs, where specific surface functionalities enhance their optical response. In addition to these findings, the QY of the N-CQDs was calculated using eq. (1), yielding an impressive value of 47.5 %. This relatively high QY signifies that the synthesized CQDs efficiently convert absorbed light into emitted fluorescence, further confirming their superior optical performance. Such a high quantum yield is particularly advantageous for applications requiring strong fluorescence, such as bioimaging, sensors, and light-emitting devices [50].

The fluorescence stability of N-CQDs was comprehensively evaluated in terms of pH sensitivity, ionic strength, and long-term storage performance. The pH-dependent fluorescence behaviour of N-CQDs was thoroughly investigated to assess the stability and detection efficiency in various pH conditions. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the FL intensity of N-CQDs exhibited significant variations across a pH range of 3–13. It was observed that the FL intensity peaked at neutral pH. At highly acidic pH values, the fluorescence intensity decreased sharply, likely due to the protonation of surface functional groups, such as amino and hydroxyl groups, which impacts their electronic properties and interactions with Bi3+ ions. Similarly, at highly basic pH, deprotonation may destabilize the N-CQDs and disrupt the quenching mechanism [51]. These findings suggest that the optimal pH range for effective interaction and quenching lies between pH 5 and 9, with the maximum intensity at neutral pH, where the functional groups remain stable and the fluorescence response is consistent. As the FL intensity peaked at neutral pH, subsequent investigations were conducted at pH 7 for optimal FL performance. In addition to pH sensitivity, N-CQDs' stability under prolonged storage conditions was also examined. The FL spectra were regularly monitored over a period of 50 days while storing the samples at 5 °C (Fig. 5b). Interestingly, only a minor variation of less than 0.5 % was detected in the FL intensity throughout the storage duration, indicating remarkable stability and preserving the photoluminescent characteristics even after an extended period. This exceptional fluorescence retention over time suggests that N-CQDs are highly stable materials, well-suited for long-term use. Furthermore, the effect of ionic strength was evaluated by progressively increasing the concentration of NaCl from 0 M to 1.5 M in increments of 0.3 M. Throughout this gradual increase in ionic strength, the FL intensity remained largely unchanged, demonstrating that N-CQDs exhibit strong resistance to salt-induced quenching (Fig. 5c). Their ability to maintain significant photoluminescence capacity even in relatively high concentrations of NaCl solutions underscores their robustness in environments with varying ionic strengths. To further assess their overall stability, N-CQDs were subjected to different temperature conditions. Their FL spectra were recorded over a temperature range of 25–65 °C, with measurements taken at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 5d). A clear trend was observed, wherein the FL intensity gradually decreased as the temperature increased, becoming particularly pronounced beyond 45 °C. This reduction in fluorescence intensity with rising temperature could be linked to an increase in the nonradiative decay rate, which diminishes quantum efficiency and, subsequently, the observed FL intensity [41].

Studies on the stability and reproducibility of N-CQDs across three independent batches were also conducted that focused on fluorescence intensity, storage time, and temperature effects (Fig. S3). The maximum emission of all the batches of N-CQDs was observed at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. However, the FL intensity for the same concentration of N-CQDs (15 μL in 10 mL DI water) exhibited batch-to-batch variations, maintaining an overall acceptable intensity profile. The effect of temperature on FL intensity was studied for all batches over a temperature range of 25–65 °C at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. A consistent trend was noted across batches, where FL intensity gradually declined with increasing temperature. This decrease became particularly pronounced beyond 45 °C, indicating a thermally induced quenching effect. To assess the long-term stability of the N-CQDs, FL spectra were monitored over a 20-day storage period at 5 °C for batches 2 and 3. Remarkably, negligible variations in FL intensity were detected in all batches throughout the storage duration, demonstrating retention of photoluminescent properties over time. Overall, these investigations reveal the multifaceted stability of N-CQDs across several environmental parameters, emphasizing their resilience and suitability for practical sensing applications in diverse conditions.

The selectivity of the N-CQDs-based fluorescent sensor was examined by observing its fluorescence response to Bi3+ ions and other interfering common metal ions (Ni2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, As5+ and Pb2+) that might be present in the actual samples. Fig. 6c–e make it clear that only Bi3+ ions can effectively quench N-CQDs' fluorescence. While Co2+ and Cu2+ exhibited slightly more pronounced quenching effects than other interfering metal ions, the sensor demonstrated exceptional selectivity towards Bi3+ ions, with significantly greater fluorescence quenching. Notably, the fluorescence remained largely unaffected by other potential interferents (Fig. 6d), underscoring the exceptional selectivity of the developed sensor towards Bi3+ ions. Fig. 6e illustrates the N-CQDs solution containing various metal ions under 365 nm UV-light irradiation. Among all the tested metal ions, Bi3+ stands out by causing the N-CQDs' fluorescence to disappear completely, indicating its highly effective quenching ability. Additionally, preliminary experiments using EDTA solution demonstrated the potential for fluorescence recovery (81.6 %), indicating the possible reusability of these N-CQDs (Text S1, Fig. S4).

To evaluate the prepared sensor's sensitivity, the FL intensity of N-CQDs was monitored in the presence of varying Bi3+ concentrations under optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 6a, the fluorescence intensity progressively decreased with increasing analyte levels. The quenching efficiency was determined by constructing a Stern-Volmer plot of F0/F versus Bi3+ concentration: F0/F = 1 + Ksv [X], as shown in Fig. 6b. Here, F0 represents the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs without quencher (Bi3+), F is the intensity with Bi3+, KSV represents the Stern−Volmer quenching constant (slope), and X is the concentration of Bi3+(μΜ) [21,41]. Linear regression analysis revealed a direct relationship between the concentration of Bi3+ ions and the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F). The resulting equation, y = 0.022x + b, demonstrated a strong correlation between these two variables with a slope value (KSV) of 0.022 and a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9954. The Limit of Detection (LOD) was computed using the following equation:
(2)
LOD = 3 σ / m
where σ is the standard deviation of the N-CQDs blank signal (n = 10) and m is the slope of the linear fit. The LOD was calculated to be 0.365 μΜ within the linear range of 0.95–61.5 μΜ. The conclusion is that the as-synthesized N-CQDs can be broadly used in the sensitive and selective detection of Bi3+ ions.

Time-resolved fluorescence studies were conducted to elucidate the quenching mechanism involved in this system. A significant decrease in the average fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs, from 9.11 ns to 1.79 ns, was observed upon the addition of Bi3+ ions (Fig. 7 a, Table S2). This pronounced reduction in lifetime strongly indicates a dynamic quenching process [52]. Additionally, the spectral overlap between the UV absorption of Bi3+ ions and the excitation spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. 7b) suggests the possibility of PET. While the overlap between the emission or excitation spectra of CQDs and the absorption spectrum of a quencher could also suggest the involvement of the Inner Filter Effect (IFE), the distinguishing factor lies in the fluorescence decay time. In the case of IFE, the decay time remains unchanged, thereby ruling out its contribution to the observed quenching mechanism [50,53]. Unlike Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET), where the emission-excitation spectral overlap is a stringent requirement, PET can occur with broader spectral overlap [54].

The substantial reduction in absorbance intensity, seen in the absorbance spectrum of N-CQDs upon the introduction of Bi3+, further suggests the formation of an electronic complex, potentially facilitating electron transfer from the N-CQDs to the Bi3+ ions (Fig. 7e). Given that Bi3+ exists in the +3 oxidation state, it presents a strong potential for facilitating PET. According to HSAB (hard-soft acid-base) principle, Bi3+ is a borderline acid that can bind strongly to hard donors like N and O especially when they are present in a soft matrix like a conjugated system [55]. Temperature-dependent studies were performed to probe the quenching mechanism further (Fig. 7c). The Stern-Volmer plots (F0/F vs. concentration) revealed an increase in the quenching constant (KSV) with rising temperature, providing additional evidence for dynamic quenching [50]. Moreover, IR spectroscopy results (Fig. 7d) revealed notable changes in the intensities of the carbonyl, amine, and amide bands following the addition of Bi3+, suggesting that the surface chemistry of the N-CQDs is affected, possibly due to the formation of coordination bonds or complexes between Bi3+ and functional groups on the N-CQDs. Fig. 7e schematically illustrates the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism in N-CQDs upon interaction with Bi3+ ions. The quenching is primarily attributed to photoinduced electron transfer (PET), as supported by FTIR data showing interactions between Bi3+ ions and functional groups like hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amides on the N-CQDs surface. Bi3+ ions, being electron deficient, accept electrons from the functional groups on the N-CQDs surface. Fluorescence lifetime studies validate dynamic quenching, with a significant reduction in lifetime from 9.11 ns to 1.79 ns? The spectral overlap between the UV–vis absorption of Bi3+ and the excitation spectrum of N-CQDs reinforces PET as the possible quenching pathway [50,52,56]. These results collectively suggest that the quenching mechanism in this system is best characterized as dynamic quenching, likely driven by Photoinduced Electron Transfer (PET) between the N-CQDs and Bi3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Bi3+ ions in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The designed sensing system was tested using real-world samples to ensure its effectiveness. Tap water and agricultural runoff from a local field were first filtered, diluted and spiked with known amounts of Bi3+ ions. The N-CQDs sensor successfully recovered these amounts, with the measured values closely matching the spiked concentrations. Recovery rates ranged from around 99.7 %–101.4 %, and RSD remained low, at a maximum of around 2 %, indicating excellent reproducibility of the measurements (Table 2 , Table S3, Fig. S5).

Subsequently, bismuth-containing samples-wastewater sample from a local valve manufacturing factory and blush from a popular cosmetic brand were also analysed. Bismuth concentrations in these samples were initially measured using FL spectroscopy with the synthesized N-CQDs and cross-validated via UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. The percentage differences between the results obtained from the two techniques were calculated and are presented in Table 3 . The small percentage differences (1.73 % and 2.54 %) show the strong agreement between the two methods. These findings demonstrate the potential of the N-CQDs sensor for environmental monitoring by highlighting its promising applicability and dependability for identifying Bi3+ ions in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application as fluorescent ink and in anti-counterfeiting
ce:section.ce:para: Anti-counterfeiting plays a crucial role in transactions, medication, and production [57]. Due to the high quantum yield and excellent fluorescence stability of the synthesized N-CQDs, an invisible fluorescent ink was developed for use in anti-counterfeiting applications. The N-CQDs solution was applied to write characters on filter paper without sample pretreatment. After drying, the characters remained completely invisible under natural light but emitted a bright blue luminescence when exposed to UV light of 365 nm (Fig. 8 a and b). This finding confirms that N-CQDs can be effectively employed as invisible fluorescent ink, offering a practical and reliable method for enhancing the security of sensitive documents and other materials.

Smartphone-based sensor devices are increasingly popular due to their affordability, safety, and user-friendly nature [58,59]. Experimental results revealed that the blue fluorescence of N-CQDs exhibits a strong linear correlation with the concentration of Bi3+ ions. To evaluate the suitability of the probe for device-based detection, RGB analysis was conducted on N-CQDs solutions with varying concentrations of Bi3+ ions under 365 nm UV radiation. As the Bi3+ concentration increased, a noticeable colour change and a gradual expansion of fluorescence quenching (darker regions) were observed (Fig. S6). An RGB-based mobile recognition approach was utilized to quantify these colour changes. RGB values of the solutions were captured using the rear camera of a smartphone equipped with a freely available mobile app (Colour Analyzer). A calibration curve was generated by plotting the G values against Bi3+ concentrations, enabling quantitative assessment. The analysis demonstrated a strong linear relationship for Bi3+ concentrations ranging from 0.3 to 11.5 μM, with an R2 value of 0.9962 and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.218 μM (Fig. 8c). The as-synthesized N-CQDs, when integrated with smartphone-based detection, offer significant potential for the rapid and reliable detection of Bi3+ ions, establishing this platform as a versatile tool for real-time and portable sensing applications.

In this study, a cost-efficient approach was developed to synthesize highly water-soluble fluorescent N-CQDs using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors, with no need for organic solvents. The N-CQDs were produced via microwave irradiation without the addition of chemical modifiers. These N-CQDs exhibited stable bright blue fluorescence with an impressive quantum yield (QY) of 47.5 %. Surface nitrogen atoms facilitated the selective detection of Bi3+ ions over other metal ions. Fluorescence quenching by Bi3+ ions allowed for highly sensitive detection, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.365 μM within the concentration range of 0.95–61.5 μM. Various spectroscopic analyses confirmed that the quenching mechanism involved dynamic quenching via the PET process. The practical utility of these N-CQDs was demonstrated in various real-world samples, such as tap water, industrial wastewater, and agricultural runoff, with a recovery rate of Bi3+ ions ranging from 99.2 % to 101.4 %. The fluorescent sensor was also integrated with a smartphone, enabling quick and efficient detection of Bi3+ ions. Moreover, these N-CQDs could be used as fluorescent ink and in anti-counterfeiting applications. With its simplicity, affordability, and sustainability, this method offers a promising tool for Bi3+ ions detection in diverse environmental applications.

Anoushka: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Manviri Rani: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision. Uma Shanker: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Dr Uma Shanker and Dr Manviri Rani are grateful to DST-SERB, New Delhi (Sanction order no. EEQ/2023/000196 & CRG/2023/002740) for funding this project. Ms Anoushka thanks the Ministry of Education (MoE) in New Delhi for a research fellowship. Characterization support from Material Research Centre MNIT Jaipur, IIC, NIT Jalandhar India, and TRFSM facility, IIT Bombay is greatly acknowledged.
